messages.subject: [R-sig-Geo] Implementing a spatially-varying anisotropic kernel in GWR using a pre-computed distance matrix
messages.subject_clean: Implementing a spatially-varying anisotropic kernel in GWR using a pre-computed distance matrix
messages.body_plain:
I am fitting a Geographically Weighted Regression (GWR) using the *GWmodel*.
The model is fitted at a coarse spatial scale and used to predict at a
finer spatial scale. My data are in a projected coordinate system (metres).
The formula is simple: one response variable, one predictor.

Instead of the standard isotropic kernel, I want a kernel whose shape
reflects the geometry of a satellite sensor's point spread function (PSF).
Specifically:

- The sensor has a cross-track viewing angle *?* that varies spatially
across the scene. I have this as a raster at the same resolution as my
response variable.
- The PSF expands in the cross-track direction as 1/cos?(*?*). At nadir (*?*
= 0) the kernel is symmetric. As *?* increases toward the swath edge, the
kernel becomes increasingly elongated in the cross-track direction.
- The cross-track direction is not axis-aligned ? it runs roughly
bottom-left to top-right in my scene.

Rather than modifying GWmodel's internal distance function, I pre-compute a
custom distance matrix and pass it via the *dMat* argument, which GWmodel
supports in *bw.gwr*(), *gwr.basic*(), and *gwr.predict*(). The distance
between any two points is computed as follows:

1. Estimate the cross-track orientation angle *?* from the spatial gradient
of the viewing angle raster.
2. For each regression point *i*, rotate the coordinate frame by ?*?* so
that the x-axis aligns with the cross-track direction.
3. Scale the cross-track component of each distance vector by 1/cos?(*??*),
where *??* is the viewing angle at point *i* extracted from the raster.
4. Compute Euclidean distance in this rotated and scaled space.

This produces an n ? n distance matrix for fitting (calibration points vs
calibration points) and an m ? n matrix for prediction (prediction points
vs calibration points), both passed as *dMat*/*dMat1*/*dMat2* to the
relevant GWmodel functions.

Is injecting a pre-computed anisotropic distance matrix via dMat
mathematically equivalent to modifying the kernel function itself, and is
this therefore a valid way to implement a spatially-varying anisotropic
kernel in GWmodel?

pacman::p_load(terra, sf, GWmodel, ggplot2, tidyterra)

# 1. Load built-in data
data(LondonHP)
proj4string(londonhp) <- CRS("+init=epsg:27700")
sp_fit     <- londonhp[, c("PURCHASE", "FLOORSZ")]
fit_coords <- coordinates(sp_fit)

# 2. Synthetic VA raster
# Values increase linearly BL->TR (30-35 deg). Alpha is known analytically:
# BL->TR diagonal = 45 deg from East, so alpha = pi/4.
bb  <- bbox(sp_fit)
buf <- 2000

va_ext <- ext(bb[1,1] - buf, bb[1,2] + buf,
              bb[2,1] - buf, bb[2,2] + buf)

va  <- rast(va_ext, resolution = 500, crs = "EPSG:27700")
xy  <- crds(va)
va_vals <- 30 + 5 * ((xy[,1] - bb[1,1]) / diff(bb[1,]) +
                       (xy[,2] - bb[2,1]) / diff(bb[2,])) / 2
values(va) <- va_vals

alpha <- pi / 4   # BL->TR diagonal, known analytically

# 3. Anisotropic distance matrix function
make_aniso_dmat <- function(data_coords, reg_coords, va_reg, alpha) {
  n_d   <- nrow(data_coords)
  n_r   <- nrow(reg_coords)
  D     <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = n_r, ncol = n_d)
  cos_a <- cos(-alpha)
  sin_a <- sin(-alpha)
  for (i in seq_len(n_r)) {
    dx      <- data_coords[, 1] - reg_coords[i, 1]
    dy      <- data_coords[, 2] - reg_coords[i, 2]
    u       <-  cos_a * dx - sin_a * dy
    v       <-  sin_a * dx + cos_a * dy
    scale_i <- 1 / cos(va_reg[i] * pi / 180)^2
    D[i, ]  <- sqrt((u * scale_i)^2 + v^2)
  }
  D
}

# 4. Extract VA at calibration points
va_fit                <- extract(va, fit_coords)[, 1]
va_fit[is.na(va_fit)] <- mean(va_fit, na.rm = TRUE)

# 5. Calibration distance matrix
dMat_fit <- make_aniso_dmat(fit_coords, fit_coords, va_fit, alpha)

# 6. Bandwidth selection
bw <- bw.gwr(
  formula  = PURCHASE ~ FLOORSZ,
  data     = sp_fit,
  approach = "AIC",
  kernel   = "gaussian",
  adaptive = TRUE,
  dMat     = dMat_fit,
  parallel.method = "omp",
  parallel.arg = 4
)

# 7. Fit GWR
gwr_fit <- gwr.basic(
  formula  = PURCHASE ~ FLOORSZ,
  data     = sp_fit,
  bw       = bw,
  kernel   = "gaussian",
  adaptive = TRUE,
  dMat     = dMat_fit,
  parallel.method = "omp",
  parallel.arg = 4
)

print(gwr_fit)

# 8. Prediction grid (500 m, FLOORSZ = mean of calibration data)
pred_rast         <- rast(va_ext, resolution = 500, crs = "EPSG:27700")
values(pred_rast) <- mean(sp_fit$FLOORSZ, na.rm = TRUE)
names(pred_rast)  <- "FLOORSZ"

pred_xy             <- as.data.frame(pred_rast, xy = TRUE, na.rm = TRUE)
names(pred_xy)[1:2] <- c("x", "y")
pred_coords         <- as.matrix(pred_xy[, c("x", "y")])

va_pred                 <- extract(va, pred_coords)[, 1]
va_pred[is.na(va_pred)] <- mean(va_pred, na.rm = TRUE)

# 9. Prediction distance matrix
dMat_pred <- make_aniso_dmat(fit_coords, pred_coords, va_pred, alpha)

# 10. Predict at fine scale
sp_pred              <- pred_xy
coordinates(sp_pred) <- ~ x + y
proj4string(sp_pred) <- CRS("+init=epsg:27700")

gwr_pred <- gwr.predict(
  formula     = PURCHASE ~ FLOORSZ,
  data        = sp_fit,
  predictdata = sp_pred,
  bw          = bw,
  kernel      = "gaussian",
  adaptive    = TRUE,
  dMat1       = t(dMat_pred),
  dMat2       = dMat_fit
)

pred_df              <- as.data.frame(gwr_pred$SDF)
pred_xy$purchase_hat <- pred_df$prediction

# 11. Residuals raster
sf_resid <- st_as_sf(gwr_fit$SDF)
r_resid  <- rast(pred_rast); values(r_resid) <- NA
r_resid[cellFromXY(r_resid, fit_coords)] <- sf_resid$residual

# 12. Predicted raster
r_pred <- rast(pred_rast); values(r_pred) <- NA
r_pred[cellFromXY(r_pred, pred_coords)]  <- pred_xy$purchase_hat

# 13. Plots
theme_night <- function() {
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(
      panel.background  = element_rect(fill = "grey12", colour = NA),
      plot.background   = element_rect(fill = "grey12", colour = NA),
      legend.background = element_rect(fill = "grey12", colour = NA),
      panel.grid        = element_blank(),
      plot.title        = element_text(colour = "grey95", face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle     = element_text(colour = "grey75"),
      text              = element_text(colour = "grey90"),
      axis.text         = element_text(colour = "grey70")
    )
}

p_va <- ggplot() +
  geom_spatraster(data = va) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "VA (deg)") +
  coord_sf(expand = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Synthetic viewing angle raster",
       subtitle = "30 deg (BL) -> 35 deg (TR)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_night()

p_pred <- ggplot() +
  geom_spatraster(data = r_pred) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno", na.value = "grey20",
                       name = "predicted\nprice (\u00a3)") +
  coord_sf(expand = FALSE) +
  labs(title    = "Anisotropic GWR ? predicted purchase price",
       subtitle = sprintf("PURCHASE ~ FLOORSZ  |  bisquare, adaptive BW =
%g", bw),
       x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_night()

p_resid <- ggplot() +
  geom_spatraster(data = r_resid) +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "RdBu", na.value = "grey20",
                       name = "residual",
                       limits = c(-1,1) * max(abs(minmax(r_resid)), na.rm =
TRUE)) +
  coord_sf(expand = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Anisotropic GWR ? residuals", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_night()

print(p_va)
print(p_pred)
print(p_resid)



The residuals look like that because londonhp is point data ? ~370 house
price observations scattered across London so when I assign them to a 500 m
raster using *cellFromXY()*, only the cells that happen to contain a data
point get a value, producing that sparse dotted pattern. With my actual
dataset, I don't have this issue.
